feed_card.headline: The eye evolved independently at least 40 times, nine of them in jellyfish alone.
teasers: An octopus eye has no blind spot
A camera eye can evolve in a geological blink
The mouse gene that grows eyes on flies

sections.content: The eye evolved independently at least 40 times, nine of them in jellyfish alone.

sections.content: An eye has appeared from scratch at least 40 separate times across the animal kingdom. Among the cnidarians, the jellyfish and their kin, it happened nine times over on its own. This is the organ Darwin singled out as his hardest case, the one he admitted seemed “absurd in the highest possible degree” to reach by small steps. Intuition says a structure that intricate is a fluke you stumble into once and then keep. Evolution treats it as routine. Eyes tend to appear wherever the ordinary ingredients are in place: light to detect, a body that gains from steering by it, and enough time for selection to work.

items: In Nilsson and Pelger's model, a flat light-sensitive patch turns into a focusing camera eye in about 364,000 generations.
For an animal that breeds once a year, that is under half a million years, a sliver against the more than 500 million years eyes have existed.
Every step along the way is itself a working eye, a little sharper than the one before, so selection never has to cross a useless gap.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 108 400 84' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'><rect x='10' y='120' width='76' height='54' rx='14'/><rect x='110' y='120' width='76' height='54' rx='14'/><rect x='210' y='120' width='76' height='54' rx='14'/><rect x='310' y='120' width='76' height='54' rx='14'/></g><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M86 147 h24'/><path d='M110 147 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/><path d='M186 147 h24'/><path d='M210 147 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/><path d='M286 147 h24'/><path d='M310 147 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/></g><g fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='48' y='152'>Patch</text><text x='148' y='152'>Cup</text><text x='248' y='152'>Pinhole</text><text x='348' y='152'>Lens eye</text></g></svg>

sections.content: How do you know an eye was built from scratch, rather than passed down from one ancient original? You look at how it is put together. Salvini-Plawen and Mayr found these eyes made from different tissues, focused by different optics, and grown along different developmental routes. Organs that truly share an ancestor tend to share a plan, and these do not. They also sit scattered across the animal tree, with eyeless relatives dotted between the lineages that see.

content.body: In 1859 Darwin looked straight at the problem. The eye, with its lens and its focus and its correction for aberration, seemed to him “absurd in the highest possible degree” to explain by small steps, and he said so in print before answering himself. For a century the eye stayed the showpiece of the argument that some organs were simply too perfect to have crept into being. Then in 1977 the zoologists Luitfried von Salvini-Plawen and Ernst Mayr did the tedious work of comparing photoreceptors across the animal kingdom, and concluded that eyes had arisen not once but at least 40 separate times. In 1994 Dan-Eric Nilsson and Susanne Pelger showed the climb was short, a few hundred thousand years from a bare patch to a focusing eye. And in 1995 Walter Gehring's lab found the twist. Switch on the eye-building master gene in the wrong place on a fruit fly, even with the mouse copy of the gene, and the fly grows eyes on its legs and wings.

key_figures.name: Ernst Mayr
key_figures.role: Put a number on the origins
key_figures.one_line: With Salvini-Plawen, he surveyed photoreceptors across the animal kingdom in 1977 and set the count of independent eye origins at 40 or more.

key_figures.name: Luitfried von Salvini-Plawen
key_figures.one_line: The zoologist whose survey of animal photoreceptors, with Mayr, established that eyes had evolved many separate times.

key_figures.name: Walter Gehring
key_figures.role: Found the shared eye master gene
key_figures.one_line: His lab showed in 1995 that the Pax6 gene switches on eyes anywhere on a fruit fly, even when the gene is borrowed from a mouse.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 118 400 74' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='55' y1='155' x2='355' y2='155' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='65' cy='155' r='5'/><circle cx='255' cy='155' r='5'/><circle cx='350' cy='155' r='5'/></g><circle cx='160' cy='155' r='6.5' fill='var(--accent)'/><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'><text x='65' y='138' fill='currentColor'>1859</text><text x='160' y='138' fill='var(--accent)'>1977</text><text x='255' y='138' fill='currentColor'>1994</text><text x='350' y='138' fill='currentColor'>1995</text></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='65' y='180' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.8'>Darwin's doubt</text><text x='160' y='180' fill='var(--accent)'>40+ origins</text><text x='255' y='180' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.8'>fast climb</text><text x='350' y='180' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.8'>master gene</text></g></svg>

content.title: The octopus built the better camera
content.body: The octopus eye is a camera, like ours: a pupil, a lens, a retina at the back. It evolved on a branch that split from ours more than 500 million years ago, back when the common ancestor had only light-sensitive patches. Build the same instrument twice from scratch. The details are bound to diverge. Our retina is installed backwards, nerves in front, so every vertebrate carries a blind spot where the optic nerve punches through. The octopus, wiring the same organ the other way round, has none.
content.image_caption: An octopus eye, a camera as capable as ours that evolved on a completely separate branch of the animal tree.

content.title: Independent eyes still share one ancient switch
content.body: Independent does not reach all the way down. Look under almost any eye and you find the same switch flipping it on, a gene called Pax6. The light sensor is built from the same ancient family of molecules too, the opsins. The organ was assembled separately, over and over, from one short list of parts. That is the puzzle. If both sit beneath every eye, the line between reinventing an eye and inheriting one blurs.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 108 400 84' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5'><rect x='16' y='120' width='104' height='54' rx='14'/><rect x='148' y='120' width='104' height='54' rx='14'/></g><rect x='280' y='120' width='104' height='54' rx='14' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M120 147 h28'/><path d='M148 147 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/><path d='M252 147 h28'/><path d='M280 147 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'><text x='68' y='152' fill='currentColor'>Mouse gene</text><text x='200' y='152' fill='currentColor'>Fly leg</text><text x='332' y='152' fill='var(--accent)'>An eye</text></g></svg>

content.title: Nature keeps reaching for the same few optical tricks
content.body: The nautilus focuses light with a pinhole and no lens at all, the way a pinhole camera does. A scallop lines the back of each eye with a curved mirror and focuses by reflection, like a small telescope. Both make sharp images. They rest on completely different physics. Michael Land counted roughly ten distinct optical designs across animals. Physics allows only a handful of ways to form an image. Lenses, mirrors, pinholes, and compound arrays of tubes have each been arrived at more than once.

sections.content: This turns the eye from a lucky accident into something nearer to an inevitability. Light is everywhere. Any lineage with cells that react to it sits at the foot of a slope that runs uphill toward an eye, and natural selection climbs that slope whenever the climb pays off. Different lineages take different routes up. An octopus and a human reach a camera eye by different routes, each wired back to front from the other. Image-forming eyes reach only six of the roughly 33 animal phyla. But three of them (the molluscs, the arthropods, and the chordates that include us) hold some 96% of all animal species. The animals that took over the living world are the ones that learned to see.

content.question: What does it mean to say eyes evolved independently at least 40 times?
options: One ancestral eye was gradually reshaped into 40 different forms
About 40 living species have eyes
Eyes arose from scratch in at least 40 lineages that did not inherit an eye from a shared ancestor
The eye is assembled from at least 40 distinct parts
content.explanation: Independent origin means the lineages did not share an eyed ancestor. Each built an eye separately, from different tissues and along different developmental routes, which is why their eyes follow different plans.

content.question: Why is the octopus eye a textbook case of convergent evolution?
options: It reached a camera design much like ours on a lineage that split from ours before either had an eye
It inherited the same eye as ours from a recent common ancestor
It is the only invertebrate eye with a lens
It works on completely different physics from a camera
content.explanation: Cephalopods and vertebrates split over 500 million years ago from an ancestor with only light-sensitive patches. Each evolved a camera eye separately, which is why the octopus even wires its retina without our blind spot.

content.question: Gehring's lab switched on the eye master gene Pax6, even the mouse version, in the wrong places on a fruit fly. The result matters because...
options: nothing happened, showing the gene works only in mammals
the fly grew a mouse eye, showing the two eyes are interchangeable organs
the fly lost its normal eyes, showing Pax6 suppresses vision
the fly grew extra fly eyes on its legs and wings, showing separately evolved eyes still share one ancient switch
content.explanation: The eyes that grew were fly eyes, not mouse eyes: the borrowed gene only flipped the fly's own eye-building program on. Eyes invented separately still answer to the same deep genetic switch.

content.question: A camera eye can evolve from a bare light-sensitive patch in a few hundred thousand years. Why does that undercut the claim that the eye is too complex to evolve?
options: It does not; a few hundred thousand years is far too short for such a change
Every intermediate is itself a working eye, so selection climbs a smooth slope instead of crossing a useless gap
It shows the eye must have been designed in advance
It applies only to simple animals like worms
content.explanation: The half-eye objection assumes the in-between stages are useless. They are not: a light patch, a cup, and a pinhole each see better than the last, so every step is favored and the whole climb is short.

content.question: Image-forming eyes occur in only six animal phyla, yet three of those hold roughly 96% of animal species. What does that pattern suggest?
options: Eyes are a rare curiosity with little consequence
About 96% of animal species are molluscs
Being able to form images is bound up with the kinds of animals that came to dominate the living world
Most animal species are completely blind
content.explanation: The phyla with image-forming eyes, the molluscs, arthropods, and chordates, are exactly the ones that radiated into most of animal life. Vision and evolutionary success travel together.

content.question: If eyes were invented separately dozens of times, what do nearly all of them still share?
options: The Pax6 switch that triggers eye-building and the opsin molecule that catches light, both from a common ancestor
An identical lens shape
The same number of photoreceptor cells
Nothing at all; they are unrelated in every respect
content.explanation: The organ was built many times, but the genetic switch that starts the process and the light-sensitive pigment inside it trace back to a shared ancestor. That is why “independent” describes the eye, not its molecular toolkit.

content.myth: The eye is too complex to have evolved by natural selection.
content.reality: Detailed models turn a bare light-sensitive patch into a focusing eye in a few hundred thousand years, each stage a working eye that sees a little better than the last. Nothing has to appear all at once.

content.myth: All animal eyes descend from a single original eye.
content.reality: The organ itself was built at least 40 separate times on different body plans. Only the switch that triggers it and the opsin that catches light trace back to one ancestor.

content.myth: An octopus eye and a human eye are basically the same eye.
content.reality: It is one design reached twice over, by two lineages that had split long before either had an eye.

content.body: The number itself is soft. Counting independent origins means deciding when a modified eye becomes a new one, and the shared Pax6 switch under nearly all of them makes that line genuinely blurry. As molecular tools improve, some groups keep splitting into more origins rather than fewer, with the jellyfish tally climbing toward nine. Whether the true figure is 40 or several times that is still open.
items: Where is the real boundary between reinventing an eye and inheriting the tools to grow one?
Does the shared Pax6 switch mean all eyes are, in some deep sense, one lineage after all?
How high does the count climb as more lineages are examined in molecular detail?

content.label: BioNumbers: eyes evolved independently 40 or more times (Salvini-Plawen and Mayr, 1977)
Nilsson DE, Pelger S. A pessimistic estimate of the time required for an eye to evolve. Proc R Soc B 1994;256:53-58
Halder G, Callaerts P, Gehring WJ. Induction of ectopic eyes by targeted expression of the eyeless gene in Drosophila. Science 1995;267:1788-1792
Cnidarian eyes as a meta-model to explore evolutionary repeatability over deep time (2025)
Land MF. The evolution of eyes: major steps. The Keeler lecture 2017. Eye 2018;32:302-313
